title: Pear and chocolate crostata
subtitle: Italian shortcrust tart with dark chocolate ganache and poached pears
excerpt: Pear and chocolate crostata is an Italian autumn tart built on a sweet shortcrust case filled with a dark chocolate ganache, topped with pears poached until just tender. The combination of bitter dark chocolate and soft sweet pear is a classic Italian pairing. The tart is served at room temperature and holds well for two days.
sourceNotes: Crostata is the Italian shortcrust tart, typically filled with jam or fruit. The combination of dark chocolate and pear (pere e cioccolato) is a classic of Italian pastry, particularly associated with northern Italian autumn baking where Williams pears reach peak season. The pasta frolla base used here is the standard sweet Italian shortcrust. This recipe is synthesised from the established Italian crostata tradition.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: The pastry case can be blind-baked a day ahead and kept at room temperature. The pears can be poached and refrigerated overnight. Assemble and bake on the day.

glossaryTerms.definition: Pasta frolla is Italian sweet shortcrust pastry made with egg yolk rather than only water. The yolk enriches the dough and makes it shorter and more biscuit-like than plain shortcrust. It is worked minimally to keep it tender. After chilling, it is firmer than British shortcrust and can be rolled and lifted without tearing. If it cracks, press the pieces back together in the tin; pasta frolla patches easily.

content.text: This tart has three elements, each straightforward on its own. The

pastry is blind baked first. The ganache sets in the baked case. The poached pears are arranged on top before the final bake that sets the ganache around them. Do not overbake the final stage: the ganache should be just set at the edges with a slight wobble in the centre when you take it from the oven.
Make the pastry and blind bake

Rub the 125 g cold butter into the 250 g flour and 80 g icing sugar until it resembles breadcrumbs. Add the 2 egg yolks and salt and mix until the dough just comes together, adding a teaspoon of cold water if needed. Flatten to a disc, wrap and refrigerate for 30 minutes. Roll out to 3 mm thickness and line a 23 cm loose-bottomed tart tin. Refrigerate for 15 minutes. Heat the oven to 180 degrees C. Line the pastry case with paper and baking beans. Blind bake for 15 minutes, then remove the beans and bake for a further 5 minutes until the base is dry and just starting to colour.
Poach the pears and make the ganache

Simmer the pear halves in a pan with the 50 g caster sugar, lemon juice and enough water to just cover for 5 to 8 minutes until a knife goes in with only slight resistance. Remove and cool on a rack. For the ganache, bring the 150 ml double cream to just below the boil. Pour over the 180 g chopped dark chocolate and 20 g butter. Leave for 2 minutes, then stir until smooth.

Pour the ganache into the blind-baked case. Arrange the drained pear halves on top, cut side down, pressing gently into the ganache. Bake at 180 degrees C for 15 to 18 minutes until the ganache is just set at the edges but still has a slight tremor in the centre. Cool for 30 minutes before slicing. Dust with icing sugar just before serving.

items.solution: The cream was too hot or the chocolate was added too quickly. Next time add the cream in two additions, stirring from the centre outward. If already split, add a tablespoon of warm cream and stir gently.
The pastry was not chilled after lining the tin, or the sides were not pressed firmly to the tin. After lining, refrigerate for at least 15 minutes before blind baking.
